elements.sent-2411-001: We study the non-hermitian Kitaev chain model, for arbitrary complex parameters.
elements.sent-2411-002: In particular, we give a concise characterisation of the curves of eigenvalues in the complex plane in the infinite size limit.
elements.sent-2411-003: Using this solution, we characterise under which conditions the skin effect is absent, and for which eigenstates this is the case.
elements.sent-2411-004: We also fully determine the region in parameter space for which the model has a zero mode.
elements.sent-2411-005: The study of non-hermitian systems, in various contexts, has been extremely intense during the last years.
elements.sent-2411-006: These studies cover the properties of non-hermitian systems in general (focussing on the differences and similarities with hermitian systems), studies of particular non-hermitian models, as well as utilising non-hermitian systems for actual applications, using various different types of physical systems \cite{ BeBuKu2019, BrLiBeRoAr2022unpub, Br2014, DeYoHa2021, Ed2022, EdKuBe2019, GoAsKaTaHiUe2018, KaBeSa2019, KaShUeSa2019, KoBu2020, KuDw2019, KuEdBuBe2018, MaBe2021, MaVaTo2018, LaQv2023, Le2016, LeLiGo2019, LeBlHuChNo2017, LiWaDuCh2024unpub, LiWeWuRuChLeNi2024unpub, LiLi2024, LiTaYaLiLe2023, LiLiAn2023, MaAuPePaAc2024, MoArBe2023, NeRo2024, OkKaShSa2020, OrHe2023, RaSiRaJi2024unpub, RiRo2022, RoOrAgHe2024, SaCiCABa2024, ShZhFu2018, StBe2021, VyRo2021, YaBe2024unpub, YaMa2023, YaMoBe2023, YaWa2018, YoPeKaHa2019, XiDeWaZhWaYiXu2019, Xi2018, ZeBe2024, ZhSoZhZhLiCaLi2024, ZhLe2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-007: One aspect that is of particular relevance for the current paper is the presence of the skin-effect, which is closely related to the breakdown of the famed bulk boundary correspondence of hermitian topological systems \cite{ HeBaRe2019, Lo2019, LeTh2019, LiZhAiGoKaUeNo2019, FlZoVaBeBaTi2022, ZiReRo2021, BoKrSl2020, YoMu2019, YaMoBe2021, EdKuYoBe2020, Sc2020, BrHy2019, EdAr2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-008: In this paper, we focus solely on Kitaev chain \cite{Ki2001 famous for its Majorana zero modes in its topological phase (see \cite{Al2012 for a review).}}
elements.sent-2411-009: We consider the non-hermitian version of this model, which has, in various incarnations, been studied before \cite{ Yu2017, KlCaDaMaWu2017, LiZhZhSo2018, LiJiSo2020, KaAsKaUe2018, Li2019, YaSo2020, ZhGuKoZhLi2021, SaNiTa2022, SaLa2023, ShSo2023, LiWaSoWa2024, RaRoKuKaSa2023, Zh2024, MoNa2024, FuKaMo2024, PiMeAg2024unpub, CaAg2024unpub, LiLiZhTiArLi2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: We concisely characterise the location of the eigenvalues in the complex plane in the limit for an open chain in the infinite system size.
elements.sent-2411-011: It is clearly natural to ask for which general choice of parameters does the non-hermitian Kitaev chain have a zero-mode.
elements.sent-2411-012: Interestingly, the full answer to this question is arguably a bit more complicated than one might expect.
elements.sent-2411-013: The eigenvalues of generic non-hermitian systems are known to be very sensitive to the boundary conditions \cite{KuEdBuBe2018,KoBu2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: This is due to the skin effect.
elements.sent-2411-015: Eigenstates exhibiting the skin effect are localised at the boundary of the system, with an amplitude that decays exponentially in the bulk of the system.
elements.sent-2411-016: Because of this exponential localisation of the eigenstates near the boundary, it is clear that the system will be sensitive to small changes in the boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-017: In addition, the usual algorithms to obtain the eigenvalues numerically becomes unstable for large system sizes.
elements.sent-2411-018: For one-dimensional systems, one can use knowledge of the periodic system in order to predict whether or not the open system exhibit skin-effect or not.
elements.sent-2411-019: In particular, one considers the eigenvalues of the periodic system, and determines if this winds around an arbitrary point \cite{GoAsKaTaHiUe2018,KaShUeSa2019,OkKaShSa2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: If such a winding exists, the system exhibits a skin effect.
elements.sent-2411-021: It is, however, not a priori clear for which choices of parameters in the model this occurs, and if it occurs, to which eigenvalues it pertains.
elements.sent-2411-022: Because of this, it is interesting to find an exact solution of the non-hermitian model one studies.
elements.sent-2411-023: By this we mean a concise characterisation of the eigenvalues, that can be easily solved numerically, without stability issues.
elements.sent-2411-024: In the current paper, we provide explicit answers to the questions we posed above for the non-hermitian Kitaev chain.
elements.sent-2411-025: We introduce the non-hermitian Kitaev chain, in order to set the notation, and start with the general analysis to solve the model in Sec.~\ref{sec:model .}
elements.sent-2411-026: We continue in Sec.~\ref{sec:finite-size by providing the solution of the non-hermitian Kitaev chain, for {\em chains of finite length , under the restriction that the left and right hopping parameters $t_1$ and $t_2$ are equal.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: In Sec.~\ref{sec:zero-modes we give a complete characterisation of the parameters, for which the (non-hermitian) model has a zero-mode.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Here, we directly work in the infinite system size limit.
elements.sent-2411-029: In Sec.~\ref{sec:Linfinity , we provide a concise characterisation of the eigenvalues of the model, again for fully generic parameters, in the infinite system size limit.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Using this characterisation, one can easily obtain the curves in the complex plain corresponding to the eigenvalues of the model.
elements.sent-2411-031: In Sec.~\ref{sec:skin-effect , we analyse the solution to determine under which conditions the model exhibits skin effect, and if this is the case, to which eigenvalues this pertains.}
elements.sent-2411-032: In Sec.~\ref{sec:discussion we briefly discuss our results.}
elements.sent-2411-033: In this section we state the model we are interested in, namely the Kitaev chain with nearest neighbour hopping and pairing, for arbitrary, complex parameters and start with the analysis to find the eigenvalues.
elements.sent-2411-034: We are mainly interested in open chains, with `free' boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-035: In this paper, we do not study the transition from the open to the periodic system.
elements.sent-2411-036: In terms of fermion creation and annihilation operators, $c_j^\dagger$, $c_j$, the model for $L$ sites reads \begin{equation}\label{eq:nh-top-sc} H = \sum_{j=1}^{L} \frac{m}{2} \bigl( c_j^\dagger c_j - c_j c_j^\dagger \bigr) + \sum_{j=1}^{L-1} \Bigl( t_1 c_j^\dagger c_{j+1} + t_2 c_{j+1}^\dagger c_j + d_1 c_j^\dagger c_{j+1}^\dagger + d_2 c_{j+1} c_{j} \Bigr) \ .\end{equation}.

elements.section-2411-004: Condition for the presence of zero modes.
elements.sent-2411-086: In this section, we determine under which conditions the model has a zero mode.
elements.sent-2411-087: We consider arbitrary complex parameters, and work directly in the $L\rightarrow\infty$ limit.
elements.sent-2411-088: In finite systems, the energy of a zero mode decays exponentially with system size, but because we work in the limit $L\rightarrow\infty$, we put $\lambda = 0$ identically.

elements.sent-2411-161: The remaning three Vieta equations for the roots can then be written as \begin{equation}\bigl( \kappa + \frac{1}{\kappa}\bigr) \bigl[ (s + \frac{1}{s}) + 2 \cos\alpha \bigr] = \frac{2 m (t_1 + t_2)}{d_1 d_2 - t_1 t_2} \nonumber \bigl( \kappa - \frac{1}{\kappa}\bigr) \bigl[ (s + \frac{1}{s}) - 2 \cos\alpha \bigr] = \frac{2 \lambda (t_1 - t_2)}{d_1 d_2 - t_1 t_2} \nonumber ( \kappa + \frac{1}{\kappa})^2 + 2 \cos\alpha (s + \frac{1}{s}) = \frac{\lambda^2 - m^2 - (t_1 + t_2)^2}{d_1 d_2 - t_1 t_2} \ . \label{eq:vieta}\end{equation} The curve(s) in the complex plane determined by the eigenvalues $\lambda$ in the limit $L \rightarrow\infty$ are obtained by varying $0 \leq \alpha < 2\pi$ (we note that $\alpha$ plays the role of the momentum $k$ in the periodic case).

elements.sent-2411-192: There is a long history of determining the location of the roots of polynomials in the complex plane and several algorithms exist to determine, say, how many roots have a negative real part, {\em without having to determine the actual roots.}
elements.sent-2411-193: Such algorithms are often used in stability analyses of various systems.
elements.sent-2411-194: For instance, the Routh–Hurwitz stability criterion \cite{Ro1877,Hu1895 determines if all the roots of a polynomial have negative real parts.}
elements.sent-2411-195: One way to derive the criterion is to construct the sequence of Sturm polynomials associated with the polynomial under investigation \cite{St1829 .}
elements.sent-2411-196: The algorithm we focus on is tailored to determine the number of roots within, on and outside of the unit circle.
elements.sent-2411-197: This is achieved by using a conformal map, that maps the imaginary axis to the unit circle.
elements.sent-2411-198: In particular, the algorithm we use is due to Bistritz \cite{Bi2002 , but also in the case of finding the number of roots inside the unit circle, the topic has a long history, dating back a century at least \cite{Co1922 .}}
elements.sent-2411-199: For a polynomial with explicit coefficients, the algorithm fully determines the number of roots of each `type'.
elements.sent-2411-200: We however, would like to determine the number of roots on the unit circle as a function of the parameters in the model.
elements.sent-2411-201: This is a harder problem, and although we believe we determined all cases for which there are at least two roots on the unit circle, we do not have a proof for this in the general case with complex parameters.
elements.sent-2411-202: The starting point of the algorithm is a polynomial of degree $n$, $P_n(x)$, and we assume that $P_n(1) = 1$.
elements.sent-2411-203: As long as $P_n(1) \neq 0$, we can always rescale $P_n(x)$ as necessary.
elements.sent-2411-204: If $P_n(1) = 0$, we can factorise out the root $x=1$.
elements.sent-2411-205: From the polynomial $P_n(x)$, a set of polynomials $T_i (x)$, of degree $i$ with $i = n, n-1,\ldots, 0$ is constructed.
elements.sent-2411-206: In this paper, we do not describe the actual algorithm to determine the polynomials $T_i (x)$, but simply state the results and refer to \cite{Bi2002 for the details.}
elements.sent-2411-207: In general, the algorithm to determine the polynomials $T_i(x)$ can be `regular' or `singular'.
elements.sent-2411-208: For now, we assume that the algorithm is `regular' and discuss the singular case below.
elements.sent-2411-209: We assume that we obtained the polynomials $T_i(x)$ explicitly.
elements.sent-2411-210: From the polynomials $T_i (x)$ one forms the sequence \begin{equation}A = \{ T_n (1), T_{n-1} (1), \ldots T_1 (1), T_0(1) \} \ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-211: The algorithm to define the $T_i (x)$ guarantees that $T_i(1)$ is real for all $i$.
elements.sent-2411-212: Therefore, we can define $\nu_n$ as the number of sign changes in the sequence $A$.
elements.sent-2411-213: The number of zeroes of $P(x)$ inside the unit circle is then given by $\alpha_n = n - \nu_n$, while the number of zeroes of $P(x)$ outside the unit circle is given by $\gamma_n = \nu_n$.

elements.sent-2411-216: In this case, the algorithm proceeds in a slightly different manner, and one obtains different polynomials $T'_{s-1 (x), T'_{s-2 (x), \ldots T'_{1 (x), T'_{0 (x)$ (which also have the property that $T'_{i (1)$ is real for all $i$).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-217: In this case, one defines \begin{equation}A = \{ T_n (1), T_{n-1} (1), \ldots, T_{s}(1), T'_{s-1}(1), T'_{s-2}(1) \ldots T'_1 (1), T'_0(1) \} B = \{ T_{s}(1), T'_{s-1}(1), T'_{s-2}(1) \ldots T'_1 (1), T'_0(1) \} \ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-218: Again, $\nu_n$ is the number of sign changes in $A$, but we now also define $\nu_s$ as the number of sign changes in $B$.
elements.sent-2411-219: In this case, the number of zeroes inside the unit circle is $\alpha_n = n - \nu_n$, the number of zeroes on the unit circle is $\beta_n = 2\nu_s - s$, while the number of zeroes outside of the unit circle is $\gamma_n = n - \alpha_n - \beta_n$.
elements.sent-2411-220: For our problem, we analyse the bulk equation, which we write as follows \begin{equation}P(x) = \frac{1}{N_2}\bigl( D_2 (1+x^4) + x^3 (- \lambda t_d - m t_s) + x (\lambda t_d - m t_s) + x^2 (N_2 + 2 m t_s -2 D_2) \Bigr) \ ,\end{equation} with \begin{equation}N_2 = \lambda^2 - (m+t_s)^2 D_2 = d_1 d_2 - t_1 t_2 t_s = t_1+t_2 t_d = t_1-t_2 \ .\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-030: \begin{equation}P(x) = \frac{1}{N_2}\bigl( D_2 (1+x^4) + x^3 (- \lambda t_d - m t_s) + x (\lambda t_d - m t_s) + x^2 (N_2 + 2 m t_s -2 D_2) \Bigr) \ ,\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-031: \begin{equation}N_2 = \lambda^2 - (m+t_s)^2 D_2 = d_1 d_2 - t_1 t_2 t_s = t_1+t_2 t_d = t_1-t_2 \ .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-221: The polynomial $P(x)$ is scaled such that $P(1) = 1$.
elements.sent-2411-222: To proceed, we note that when the system does not exhibit skin effect, the eigenvalues $\lambda$ of the {\em open chain that we study lie on the curve given by the eigenvalues of the {\em periodic chain .}}
elements.sent-2411-223: We use this information when analysing the sequences $A$ and $B$ defined above.
elements.sent-2411-224: The eigenvalues for the periodic chain are given by Eq.~\eqref{eq:lambda-periodic with $0 \leq k < 2\pi$.}
elements.sent-2411-225: Because the solution in the periodic case satisfies the same bulk equation, we find that $x = e^{i k}$ is a root of $P(x)$ provided that we set $\lambda = \lambda_+(k)$.
elements.sent-2411-226: Similarly, $x = e^{-i k}$ is a root of $P(x)$ for $\lambda = \lambda_-(k)$.
elements.sent-2411-227: In both cases, at least one of the roots lies on the unit circle, implying that the Bistritz algorithm is singular at some level (when $\lambda$ is set to $\lambda_\pm (k)$).
elements.sent-2411-228: For this reason, we should analyse at which level the algorithm is singular, depending on the parameters of the model.
elements.sent-2411-229: If we find that the algorithm is singular at level $s$, with $s$ even, we know that the number of zeroes on the unit circle, given by $\beta_n = 2\nu_s - s$ is also even.
elements.sent-2411-230: This implies that there are at least two zeroes on the unit circle, because we know that there is at least one such zero.
elements.sent-2411-231: This in turn implies the absence of the skin effect.
elements.sent-2411-232: Because the product of the roots $x_1 x_2 x_3 x_4 = 1$, we also know that is it not possible to have precisely three roots on the unit circle.
elements.subsection-2411-003: Skin effect for the model with real parameters.
elements.sent-2411-233: In the case of polynomials with complex parameters, the Bistritz algorithm becomes cumbersome, because constructing the polynomials $T_i (x)$ involves taking the complex conjugate.
elements.sent-2411-234: Therefore, we initially focus on the case with real parameters $m, t_1, t_2, d_1, d_2$, but of course allow $\lambda$, and hence $N_2$, to be complex.
elements.sent-2411-235: In this case, we obtain the following results for the polynomials $T_i (x)$.
elements.formula-2411-032: \begin{equation}\nonumber T_4 (x) = 2 \Bigl( D_2 \Re(N_2) (1-2x^2+x^4) - m t_s \Re(N_2) (x-2x^2+x^3) + i t_d \Im(\lambda N_2^*) (x - x^3) \Bigr) / (N_2 N_2^*) + 2x^2 T_4 (1) = 2 T_4 (0) = 2D_2 \Re(N_2) / (N_2 N_2^*) \ . \nonumber\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-033: \begin{equation}\nonumber T_3 (x) = 2\Bigl(i D_2 \Im(N_2) (1 + x - x^2 - x^3) - i m t_s \Im(N_2) (x-x^2) - t_d \Re(\lambda N_2^*) (x+x^2) \Bigr) / (N_2 N_2^*) T_3 (1) = -4 \Re(\lambda N_2^*) / (N_2 N_2^*) T_3 (0) = 2 i D_2 \Im (N_2) / (N_2 N_2^*) \ . \nonumber\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-034: \begin{equation}\nonumber T_2 (x) = -2 t_d \Re(\lambda) (1-x^2) / (i \Im(N_2)) -2 x T_2 (1) = -2 T_2 (0) = -2 t_d \Re(\lambda)/(i \Im(N_2)) \ . \nonumber\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-035: \begin{equation}\nonumber N_2 N_2^* \, T_1 (x) = 2\Bigl( - D_2 \Im(N_2)^2 / (t_d \Re(\lambda)) + t_d \Re(\lambda N_2^*) \Bigr)(1+x) + 2 i m t_s \Im (N_2) (1-x) N_2 N_2^* \, T_1 (1) = -4 D_2 \Im(N_2)^2 / (t_d \Re(\lambda)) + 4 t_d\Re(\lambda N_2^*) N_2 N_2^* \, T_1 (0) = -2D_2 \Im(N_2)^2 / (t_d \Re(\lambda)) + 2 t_d \Re(\lambda N_2^*) +2 i m t_s \Im(N_2) \ . \nonumber\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-237: We start by analysing under which conditions $T_3 (x) \equiv 0$.
elements.sent-2411-239: The first condition $N_2 = N_2^*$ is equivalent to $\Re(\lambda) = 0$ or $\Im(\lambda) = 0$.
elements.sent-2411-240: The second condition $(\lambda + \lambda^*) t_d =0$ is equivalent to $\Re(\lambda) = 0$ or $t_d = 0$.
elements.sent-2411-241: Combined, we find that $T_3 (x) \equiv 0$ requires that either $\Re(\lambda) = 0$ or that $\Im(\lambda) = t_d = 0$.
elements.sent-2411-242: We remark that $\Re(\lambda) = 0$ implies that $4 d_1 d_2 \sin(k)^2 + (m + (t_1+t_2)\cos(k) )^2 \leq 0$, which can only occur if $d_1$ and $d_2$ have opposite signs (or when $d_1 d_2 = 0$).
elements.sent-2411-243: On the other hand, if $\Im(\lambda) = 0$ and $t_d = 0$ we need $4 d_1 d_2 \sin(k)^2 + (m + (t_1+t_2)\cos(k) )^2 \geq 0$.
elements.sent-2411-244: Finally, we note that the explicit form of $\lambda_\pm(k)$ implies that for $t_d = 0$, we have that either $\Re(\lambda) = 0$ or $\Im (\lambda) = 0$.
elements.sent-2411-245: Hence, for $t_d = 0$ the algorithm is singular at level $s=4$ (we recall that we assumed that all the parameters of the model are real).
elements.sent-2411-246: This finishes the analysis of the conditions $T_3 (x) \equiv 0$.
elements.sent-2411-247: The other way in which we can have two roots on the unit circle is when the algorithm is singular at level 2, that is when $T_1 (x) \equiv 0$ (and $T_2(0) \neq 0$).
elements.sent-2411-248: By analysing the form of $T_1 (x)$, making use of the explicit form of the eigenvalues in the periodic case $\lambda_\pm (k)$, one finds that $T_1 (x) \propto m t_s t_d$.
elements.sent-2411-249: Because $T_2(0) \neq 0$ for $m=0$ or $t_s = 0$, we obtain that the algoritm is also singular when either $m=0$ or $t_s = 0$.
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{tabular}{c | c} condition on parameters condition on $k$ $m = 0$ $\forall k$ $t_1 = t_2$ $\forall k$ $t_2 = -t_2$ $\forall k$ $d_1 d_2 < 0$ $4 d_1 d_2 \sin(k)^2 + (m + (t_1+t_2)\cos(k) )^2 < 0 $ \end{tabular} \caption{Absence of the skin effect, in the case of real parameters} \label{tab:no-skin-effect-real}
elements.sent-2411-250: We summarise the result in table \ref{tab:no-skin-effect-real .}
elements.sent-2411-251: For the model with real parameters, there is no skin effect when either $m = 0$, or when $t_1 = \pm t_2$.
elements.sent-2411-252: In addition, there is no skin effect when $\Re(\lambda) = 0$, which occurs (over an extended range for $k$) when $4 d_1 d_2 \sin(k)^2 + (m + (t_1+t_2)\cos(k) )^2 < 0$, requiring $d_1 d_2 < 0$ (we note that $|d_1 d_2|$ should be sufficiently large in order to have $4 d_1 d_2 \sin(k)^2 + (m + (t_1+t_2)\cos(k) )^2 < 0$).
elements.sent-2411-253: We note that the eigenvalues being real ($\Im(\lambda) = 0$) alone does not imply that the skin effect is absent.
elements.sent-2411-254: Real eigenvalues can have skin effect when $t_1 \neq t_2$.
elements.sent-2411-255: To illustrate these results, we plot the (complex) spectrum of the model for several parameters.
elements.sent-2411-256: In all the plots, we use the following colour conventions.
elements.sent-2411-257: The gray curves represent the eigenvalues of the model with {\em periodic boundary conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-258: The green dots represent the eigenvalues of the open chain of finite length with $L = 100$ sites.
elements.sent-2411-259: The blue lines represent eigenvalues of the infinite open chain, corresponding to eigenstates that {\em do have skin effect.}
elements.sent-2411-260: Finally, the red lines represent eigenvalues of the infinite open chain, corresponding to eigenstates that {\em do not have skin effect.}
elements.sent-2411-261: The latter eigenvalues also correspond to eigenvalues of the periodic chain.

elements.subsection-2411-004: Skin effect for the model with complex parameters.
elements.sent-2411-270: In this section, we consider the model with complex parameters, generalising the results of the previous section.
elements.sent-2411-271: As in the case of real parameters, we do not try to find all isolated points for which the eigenstates do not show a skin effect (this could occur when the curves describing the eigenvalues in the periodic case, i.e., $\lambda_\pm (k)$, self intersect).
elements.sent-2411-272: In the previous section, we obtained that $m = 0$, $t_1 = t_2$ and $t_1 = - t_2$ are three different sufficient conditions implying that there is no skin effect for all the eigenstates in the case of real parameters.
elements.sent-2411-273: We now argue that these three conditions remain sufficient in the case of complex parameters.
elements.sent-2411-274: To show this, we consider the Vieta Eqs.~\eqref{eq:vieta , and solve them for $\kappa$, $s$ and $\lambda$ under the conditions $m = 0$, $t_1 = -t_2$ or $t_1 = t_2$.}
elements.sent-2411-275: When either $m = 0$ or $t_1 = -t_2$, we find that there are solutions with $\kappa = \pm i$.
elements.sent-2411-276: When $t_1 = t_2$, there are solutions with $\kappa = \pm 1$.
elements.sent-2411-277: Because in all these cases, $|\kappa| = 1$ we find that the ordering of the roots satisfies $|x_1| \geq |x_2| = |x_3| \geq |x_4|$, which shows that the obtained solutions correspond to actual eigenvalues of the model.
elements.sent-2411-278: Because $| \kappa | = 1$, the eigenstates do not exhibit a skin effect.
elements.sent-2411-279: Indeed, in these cases the form of $\lambda$ as obtained from the Vieta equations corresponds to the eigenvalues of the periodic case, $\lambda_\pm (k)$, which has to be true in the absence of the skin effect.
elements.sent-2411-280: We continue by generalising the results for real parameters, that originated from the condition $T_3 (x) \equiv 0$.
elements.sent-2411-281: In order to do this, we need the form of the Bistritz polynomials for complex parameters.
elements.sent-2411-282: Because these are quite involved, we state them in Appendix~\ref{app:bistritz-complex .}
elements.sent-2411-283: In particular, we need $T_3(x)$ as given in Eq.~\eqref{eq:t3-complex .}
elements.sent-2411-284: We find that the condition $T_3 (x) \equiv 0$ is equivalent to the following conditions on the parameters in the model \begin{equation}\Im (D_2 N_2^*) = 0 \Im (m t_s N_2^*) = 0 \Re (\lambda t_d N_2^*) = 0 \ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-285: Let us denote the argument of $m$, $t_s$, etc. by $\phi_{m}$, $\phi_{t_s}$, etc., then the conditions above reduce to \begin{equation}\label{eq:phase-relations} \phi_{D_2} = \phi_{N_2} \bmod \pi \phi_{m} + \phi_{t_s} = \phi_{N_2} \bmod \pi \phi_{\lambda} + \phi_{t_d} = \phi_{N_2} + \pi/2 \bmod \pi \ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-286: We note that $N_2$ is not independent of the parameters.
elements.sent-2411-287: Making use of the explicit form of $\lambda$ in the periodic case, we obtain \begin{equation}N_2 = \lambda^2 - (m+t_s)^2 = 2 m t_s \bigl( \cos (k) -1 \bigr) + 4 D_2 \sin (k)^2 + 2 i \lambda t_d \sin (k) \ ,\end{equation} which implies that the relations Eq.~\eqref{eq:phase-relations are satisfied, provided that $ \phi_{D_2 = \phi_{m + \phi_{t_s = \phi_{\lambda + \phi_{t_d + \pi/2 \bmod \pi $.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-288: To continue, we assume that $\phi_{m = \phi_{D_2 - \phi_{t_s \bmod \pi$ and $\phi_{\lambda = \phi_{D_2 - \phi_{t_d - \pi/2 \bmod \pi$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-290: Because we are interested in extended regions in $k$ for which there is no skin effect, we obtain that $\phi_{t_d = \phi_{t_s \bmod \pi/2$, and $\phi_{D_2 = 2 \phi_{t_s = 2 \phi_{t_d \bmod \pi$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-291: Because of the relation $D_2 = d_1 d_2 - t_1 t_2$, we can replace the relation $\phi_{D_2 = 2 \phi_{t_s \bmod \pi$ by $\phi_{d_1 d_2 = 2 \phi_{t_s \bmod \pi$, where $\phi_{d_1 d_2}$ is the phase of $d_1 d_2$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-292: Combined, we find the following conditions, which are necessary in order that the Bistritz algorithm is singular at level 4 \begin{equation}\phi_m = \phi_{t_s} \bmod \pi \phi_\lambda = \phi_{t_d} + \pi/2 \bmod \pi \phi_{d_1 d_2} = 2\phi_{t_s} \bmod \pi \phi_{t_d} = \phi_{t_s} \bmod \pi/2 \ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-293: We still need to check when these conditions are compatible with the explicit form of the eigenvalues in the periodic case, $\lambda_\pm(k)$ as given in Eq.~\eqref{eq:lambda-periodic .}
elements.sent-2411-294: By analysing the form of $\lambda_\pm(k)$, taking the phase relations into account, one finds that the form of $d_1 d_2$ is crucial.
elements.sent-2411-295: Generically, we write $d_1 d_2 = |d_1 d_2 | e^{i \phi_{d_1 d_2 }$.}
elements.sent-2411-296: In Table~\ref{tab:no-skin-effect , we state the conditions such that the eigenstates do not show a skin effect (due to $T_3 (x) \equiv 0$), resulting from this analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-297: We note that the third line also follows from the analysis of the case with real parameters in the previous subsection and the result that phase-rotating the parameters of the model according to Eq.~\eqref{eq:paramters-phase-rotation leads to a rigid phase rotation of the spectrum.}
elements.figure-2411-006: \begin{tabular}{c | c | c | c | c | c} $t_s$ $t_d$ $m$ $d_1 d_2$ $\lambda$ absence skin effect $ |t_s| e^{i \phi_{t_s}} $ $\pm i |t_d| e^{i \phi_{t_s}}$ $\pm |m| e^{i \phi_{t_s}}$ $+ | d_1 d_2 | e^{2i \phi_{t_s}} $ $\pm |\lambda | e^{i \phi_{t_s}}$ $\forall k$ $ |t_s| e^{i \phi_{t_s}} $ $\pm i |t_d| e^{i \phi_{t_s}}$ $\pm |m| e^{i \phi_{t_s}}$ $- | d_1 d_2 | e^{2i \phi_{t_s}} $ $\pm |\lambda| e^{i \phi_{t_s}}$ $ -4 | d_1 d_2 | \sin(k)^2 + (\pm |m| + |t_s| \cos(k))^2 > 0 $ $ |t_s| e^{i \phi_{t_s}} $ $\pm |t_d| e^{i \phi_{t_s}}$ $\pm |m| e^{i \phi_{t_s}}$ $- | d_1 d_2 | e^{2i \phi_{t_s}} $ $\pm i |\lambda| e^{i \phi_{t_s}}$ $ -4 | d_1 d_2 | \sin(k)^2 + (\pm |m| + |t_s| \cos(k))^2 < 0 $ \end{tabular} \caption{Sufficient conditions for absence of the skin effect (due to $T_3 (x) \equiv 0$).} \label{tab:no-skin-effect}
elements.sent-2411-298: An obvious check on these results is to consider the hermitian case, with $N_2, \lambda, m, D_2, t_s$ all real, and $t_d$ purely imaginary.
elements.sent-2411-299: In this case, one finds that indeed $T_3 (x) \equiv 0$ implying that the Bistritz algorithm is singular at level 4.
elements.sent-2411-300: This in turn implies that the eigenstates do not have a skin effect, as expected.
elements.sent-2411-301: The other way in which the skin effect is absent, is when $T_1 (x) \equiv 0$.
elements.sent-2411-302: The coefficients of the polynomial $T_1(x)$ are much more involved, see Eq.~\eqref{eq:t1-complex .}
elements.sent-2411-303: We therefore do not attempt to fully characterise for which (complex) parameters of the model one has $T_1 (x) \equiv 0$.
elements.sent-2411-304: However, above we argued based on the Vieta equations that for either $m=0$, $t_s = 0$, or $t_d = 0$, the eigenstates do not show a skin effect.
elements.sent-2411-305: This means that under these conditions $T_1(x) \equiv 0$ even when the other parameters are complex.
elements.sent-2411-306: We are interested in generic results, that is, extended regions of the curves of eigenvalues, for which the skin effect is absent.
elements.sent-2411-307: We believe that the conditions provided, exhaust all these cases.
elements.sent-2411-308: The argument in favour of this statement is that we need that $T_1 (x) \equiv 0$ with $\lambda = \lambda_\pm (k)$, for an extended range of $k$.
elements.sent-2411-309: Due to the form of $\lambda_\pm (k)$, this only seems possible when the various terms of $\lambda_\pm (k)$ have the same argument, or when one or more of the parameters is zero.
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{plot4x.pdf} \caption{The eigenvalues for a set including complex parameters, namely $m = 2i/5$, $t_1 = 2+i$, $t_2 = 1$, $d_1 = -d_2 = \sqrt{3}$. The colour coding is the same as in Fig.~\ref{fig:case1}. } \label{fig:plot-complex}

elements.sent-2411-311: In this generic case, all the generic eigenstates exhibit the skin effect.
elements.sent-2411-312: As expected, the spectrum is significantly more complex compared to the cases we showed with real parameters.
elements.sent-2411-313: We note that the gray curves of the eigenvalues $\lambda_\pm(k)$ in the periodic case intersect themselves.
elements.sent-2411-314: The eigenvalues of the open chain in the large system size limit (given by the blue curves) cross these intersection points.
elements.sent-2411-315: This means that the eigenstates corresponding to these (six) special eigenvalues do not have a skin effect.
elements.sent-2411-316: We checked this behaviour explicitly, by solving the bulk Eq.~\eqref{eq:bulk-x , confirming that two solutions for $x$ indeed have modulus one.}
elements.sent-2411-317: In addition, we checked that the Bistritz polynomial $T_1 (x) \equiv 0$ for the given parameters and the eigenvalue $\lambda$.
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{discussion-1.pdf} \caption{Solutions for $\lambda$ of the Vieta equations, for $m = 2/5$, $t_1 = 2+i$, $t_2 = 1$, $d_1 = -d_2 = \sqrt{3}$. Only the black lines correspond to actual eigenvalues. For the blue and dark-yellow lines, the solutions $x_i$ of the bulk equation correspond to one of the branches that do not correspond to actual eigenvalues.} \label{fig:discussion-1}
elements.sent-2411-318: We studied the non-hermitian Kitaev chain for general complex parameters, pushing analytical methods as far as possible.
elements.sent-2411-319: We now discuss our results, by means of an example.
elements.sent-2411-320: We use the chain with parameters $m = 2/5$, $t_1 = 2+i$, $t_2 = 1$, $d_1 = -d_2 = \sqrt{3}$ for this purpose.
elements.sent-2411-321: One of the main results we obtained in this paper, is the characterisation of the eigenvalues of the infinite size system, in terms of three Vieta Eqs.
elements.sent-2411-322: The eigenvalue curves $\lambda$ are obtained by varying $0 \leq \alpha < 2 \pi$, but only those $\lambda$ that correspond to solutions that satisfy $1/|s| \leq |\kappa|^2 \leq |s|$ are actual eigenvalues of the model as explained in Sec.~\ref{sec:Linfinity .}
elements.sent-2411-323: In Fig.~\ref{fig:discussion-1 , we show the eigenvalues of the model with parameters $m = 2/5$, $t_1 = 2+i$, $t_2 = 1$, $d_1 = -d_2 = \sqrt{3}$ as an illustration.}
elements.sent-2411-324: The actual eigenvalues (the black lines) form a rather intricate pattern, which can be explained in terms of the three different branches of solutions of the Vieta equations.
elements.sent-2411-325: Fig.~\ref{fig:discussion-1 also shows the other two branches (the blue and yellow lines), that do not correspond to eigenvalues of the model.}
elements.sent-2411-326: It is interesting to note the regions where the black lines `intersect'.
elements.sent-2411-327: Here, the eigenvalues do not cross, nor do they repel, but form a rhombic structure.
elements.sent-2411-328: The reason why having a concise characterisation for the infinite system size model is useful, is that (as is well known) obtaining the eigenvalues for large, non-hermitian systems showing skin-effect is numerically unstable.
elements.sent-2411-329: We illustrate this using the same example, by plotting the eigenvalues, as obtained using {\em machine precision diagonalisation for system sizes $L = 100$, $L=200$, $L=400$ and $L=800$.}
elements.sent-2411-330: The results are shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:discussion-2 as the green dots, together with the infinite system size results.}
elements.sent-2411-331: We clearly see that for sizes $L=100$ and $L=200$, the eigenvalues closely follow the black lines corresponding to the infinite size model, with only minor difference due to the finite size effects.
elements.sent-2411-332: However, already for $L=400$, there is a region where the finite size eigenvalues deviate substantially from curves for infinite size.
elements.sent-2411-333: Moreover, the `eigenvalues' in this region do not satisfy particle-hole symmetry (dictating that if $\lambda$ is an eigenvalue, so is $-\lambda$), which clearly indicates that these values obtained by the diagonalisation algorithm are incorrect.
elements.sent-2411-335: In principle, one can obtain the correct eigenvalues even for these larger system sizes, if one uses a diagonalisation algorithm employing higher precision arithmetic, but in practise, this will be much slower that obtaining the infinite system size results.

elements.sent-2411-343: Finally, we studied under which conditions, the non-hermitian Kitaev chain has a zero mode (for the hermitian Kitaev chain, this corresponds to the region where the model is in the topological phase).
elements.sent-2411-344: It turns out that this region has perhaps a more complicated structure than one would expect.
elements.sent-2411-345: Namely, the model has a zero mode when the following condition is satisfied, \begin{equation}|\Im (\arccos \bigl( -\frac{m}{2 \sqrt{t_1 t_2 - d_1 d_2}} \bigr)) | < |\Im (\arccos\bigl( \frac{t_1 + t_2 }{2 \sqrt{t_1 t_2 - d_1 d_2}} \bigr))| \ .\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-362: In this section we state the model we are interested in, namely the Kitaev chain \cite{Ki2001 with nearest neighbour hopping and pairing, for arbitrary, complex parameters and start with the analysis to find the eigenvalues.}
elements.sent-2411-363: If the model were hermitian, $m$ would take the role of the chemical potential, $t_1$ and $t_2$ would be the hopping parameters, while $d_1$ and $d_2$ would correspond to the superconducting order parameter.
elements.sent-2411-364: The structure of the powers of $x$ is the standard ansatz used in solving the type of recurrence relations for the bulk of systems with periodicity.
elements.sent-2411-365: In the physics literature, this dates back at least the seminal paper of Lieb, Schultz and Mattis \cite{LiScMa1961 .}
elements.sent-2411-366: Using this ansatz, the `bulk' equations take the following form, \begin{equation}\label{eq:bulk-gen} t_2 -a d_1 +(m-\lambda) x + (t_1 + a d_1) x^2 = 0 d_2 - a t_1 + (-m - \lambda) ax + (- d_2 - a t_2) x^2 = 0 \ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-367: These eigenvalues corresponding to the model with periodic boundary conditions are obtained by first performing a (discrete) Fourier transform.

elements.sent-2411-372: Before continuing our analysis, we quickly explain the Vieta equations.
elements.sent-2411-373: For ease of presentation, we do this for a fourth order polynomial, as the generalisation to the arbitrary case is clear.
elements.sent-2411-374: We write the polynomial in terms of its coefficients $a_i$ and its roots $r_j$ as follows \begin{equation}P(x) = a_4 x^4 + a_3 x^3 + a_2 x^2 + a_1 x + a_0 = a_4 \prod_{j=1}^{4} (x-r_j) \ .\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-049: \begin{equation}P(x) = a_4 x^4 + a_3 x^3 + a_2 x^2 + a_1 x + a_0 = a_4 \prod_{j=1}^{4} (x-r_j) \ .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-375: Expanding the right hand side, and comparing with the coefficients leads to the following equations \begin{equation}r_1 + r_2 + r_3 + r_4 = -a_3/a_4 r_1 r_2 + r_1 r_3 + r_1 r_4 + r_2 r_3 + r_2 r_4 + r_3 r_4 = a_2/a_4 r_1 r_2 r_3 + r_1 r_2 r_4 + r_1 r_3 r_4 + r_2 r_3 r_4 = -a_1/a_4 r_1 r_2 r_3 r_4 = a_0/a_4 \ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-376: These are the Vieta equations, which relate the coefficients of a polynomial to its roots.
elements.sent-2411-377: We note that the left hand sides correspond to the elementary symmetric polynomials evaluated at the roots of the original polynomial $P(x)$.
elements.sent-2411-378: In the case at hand we have $a_0 = a_4$, leading to the fourth Vieta equation $x_1 x_2 x_3 x_4 = 1$ as stated above.
elements.sent-2411-379: The remaning three Vieta equations for the roots can then be written (after taking a linear combination) as \begin{equation}\bigl( \kappa + \frac{1}{\kappa}\bigr) \bigl[ (s + \frac{1}{s}) + 2 \cos\alpha \bigr] = \frac{2 m (t_1 + t_2)}{d_1 d_2 - t_1 t_2} \nonumber \bigl( \kappa - \frac{1}{\kappa}\bigr) \bigl[ (s + \frac{1}{s}) - 2 \cos\alpha \bigr] = \frac{2 \lambda (t_1 - t_2)}{d_1 d_2 - t_1 t_2} \nonumber ( \kappa + \frac{1}{\kappa})^2 + 2 \cos\alpha (s + \frac{1}{s}) = \frac{\lambda^2 - m^2 - (t_1 + t_2)^2}{d_1 d_2 - t_1 t_2} \ . \label{eq:vieta}\end{equation} The curve(s) in the complex plane determined by the eigenvalues $\lambda$ in the limit $L \rightarrow\infty$ are obtained by varying $0 \leq \alpha < 2\pi$ (we note that $\alpha$ plays the role of the momentum $k$ in the periodic case).
elements.sent-2411-380: Despite the fact that there are two non-physical branches, that do not lead to actual eigenvalues of the model, these non-physical branches play an important role in explaining the geometry of the actual curves the eigenvalues lie on.
elements.sent-2411-381: Without the additional constraint that the roots need to satisfy $|x_1| \geq |x_2| = |x_3| \geq |x_4|$, one would obtain smooth curves for the eigenvalues in the complex plane.
elements.sent-2411-382: With the additional necessary constraint however, one finds that the actual curves on which the eigenvalues lie exhibit branching points.
elements.sent-2411-383: This is because these actual curves `jump from one branch to another'.

elements.sent-2411-389: Such a system is stable, if the output is bounded, even in the limit when the evolved time goes to infinity.
elements.sent-2411-390: This is the case when all the roots of the characteristic polynomial corresponding to the system have negative real parts.

elements.sent-2411-399: We now summarise our results, by means of an example.
elements.sent-2411-400: The eigenvalue curves $\lambda$ are obtained by varying $0 \leq \alpha < 2 \pi$, and obtaining the solution of the three Vieta Eqs.. \eqref{eq:vieta .}
elements.sent-2411-401: A priori, the solutions of these equations are continuous and smooth as a function of the parameter $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-402: Importantly, only those $\lambda$ that correspond to solutions that satisfy $1/|s| \leq |\kappa|^2 \leq |s|$ are actual eigenvalues of the model as explained in Sec.~\ref{sec:Linfinity .}
elements.sent-2411-403: This explains the observed branched structure of the actual eigenvalues.
elements.sent-2411-404: We saw that having a concise characterisation of the eigenvalues of the infinite system size explains the structure of the curves the eigenvalues lie on.
elements.sent-2411-405: There is another reason why having a concise characterisation for the infinite system size model is useful, apart from that its interesting in its own right.
elements.sent-2411-406: Namely, as is well known, obtaining the eigenvalues for large, non-hermitian systems showing skin-effect is often numerically unstable.

elements.sent-2411-407: We studied the non-hermitian Kitaev model for arbitrary, complex parameters.
elements.sent-2411-408: By using a novel method, we obtained a concise characterisation of the eigenvalues in the thermodynamic limit for arbitrary complex parameters.
elements.sent-2411-409: Using this method, we explained the branched structure of the eigenvalues in the complex plane.
elements.sent-2411-410: In addition, we used the solution to obtain for which parameters, the model exhibit a skin effect, and if so, to which eigenvalues this pertains.
elements.sent-2411-411: For real parameters, we obtained this in full, while for arbitrary complex parameters, we obtained sufficient conditions, which we believe are also necessary.
elements.sent-2411-412: We fully characterised the parameters for which the model exhibits a zero mode.
elements.sent-2411-413: Finally, we discussed the stability issues that arise when one tries to numerically obtain the eigenvalues for large systems.
elements.sent-2411-414: Despite the fact that non-hermitian one-dimensional models have been studied in great detail, it would be interesting to apply our method to characterise the eigenvalues in the thermodynamic limit to other, more complicated systems.
elements.sent-2411-415: One direct extension of the model we consider here, is to add terms that interpolate between open and periodic boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-416: Considering different models, one can think of systens with larger unit cells and/or longer range interactions.
elements.sent-2411-417: These results generalise the Bistritz polynomials given in Sec.~\ref{sec:skin-effect for real parameters (and complex eigenvalues $\lambda$).}
elements.sent-2411-418: We refer to that section for more details.
elements.sent-2411-419: More details on the Bistritz algorithm can be found in \cite{Bi2002 .}
